Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper identifies CYP722C as a cytochrome P450 and concludes that CYP722C is a novel orobanchol synthase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: VuCYP722C is explicitly described as a CYP candidate, i.e., a cytochrome P450 of the CYP722C family.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Cowpea is explicitly given as Vigna unguiculata.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Materials and Methods state cowpea plants were V. unguiculata cv. B301.

Applied_Species.reason: The gene was analyzed in cowpea.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is cowpea, explicitly Vigna unguiculata.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The cowpea used was cv. B301.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: VuCYP722C was investigated as part of strigolactone biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The study specifically links CYP722C to orobanchol formation, i.e., the canonical strigolactone/orobanchol branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Results place VuCYP722C downstream of CLA formation and at the BC-ring closure step yielding canonical SL orobanchol.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Recombinant VuCYP722C was incubated with rac-CLA and converted it to products including orobanchol.

Primary_Product.reason: The enzyme produced orobanchol and 2'-epi-orobanchol from CLA, and a CLA+16 compound consistent with putative 18-hydroxy-CLA was also detected.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper explicitly states direct conversion of CLA to orobanchol without passing through 4DO and proposes oxidation at C-18 followed by BC-ring closure.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: VuCYP722C directly forms the target end product orobanchol from CLA in vitro.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper calls CYP722C a key enzyme and a novel orobanchol synthase, but it does not explicitly establish the step as rate-limiting or a control step.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The terminal metabolite linked to VuCYP722C is orobanchol.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Orobanchol is discussed as a canonical strigolactone, and SLs are described as carotenoid-derived phytohormones/rhizosphere signals.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper states SLs function in rhizosphere signaling for arbuscular mycorrhizal fungi and stimulate germination of root parasitic weeds; orobanchol is part of this SL context.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Orobanchol was quantified in root exudates of cowpea.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: qRT-PCR showed VuCYP722C was up-regulated in cowpea roots under Pi-deficient conditions.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The same cowpea root expression result under Pi deficiency supports this field.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The relevant condition was phosphate deficiency in hydroponic culture.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The enzyme was assayed in microsomal fractions from insect cells, but native subcellular localization of VuCYP722C in plant cells was not directly shown.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Arabidopsis NADPH-CYP reductase was explicitly used in the reconstituted in vitro enzyme assay with VuCYP722C.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The paper used RNA-seq/coexpression, qRT-PCR, heterologous expression in insect cells, and in vitro LC-MS/MS enzyme assays for VuCYP722C.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The recombinant enzyme directly produced orobanchol from CLA, and 4DO was shown not to be an intermediate in this route.

Omics_Data.reason: The cowpea RNA-seq data are explicitly stated to be deposited under DDBJ accession DRA008222.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each listed point matches Results: RNA-seq/coexpression identification, Pi-deficiency induction, direct conversion of CLA to orobanchol and 2'-epi-orobanchol with CLA+16 intermediate, and no conversion of 4DO to orobanchol.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper discusses potential agricultural value of CYP722C deficiency mainly from tomato knockout data, not direct cowpea engineering evidence.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: No cowpea in planta mutant was tested in this study, so stating that tradeoffs are NA in cowpea is consistent with the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The proposed mechanism includes 18-hydroxy-CLA/18-oxo-CLA and non-stereoselective BC-ring closure, and the paper notes ent-2'-epi-orobanchol was formed in vitro but not identified in cowpea or tomato.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Materials and Methods explicitly state synthetic SlCYP722C (Solyc02g084930.3) cDNA was obtained.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The study identifies SlCYP722C as a cytochrome P450 CYP722C functioning as orobanchol synthase.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: SlCYP722C is explicitly a CYP722C family cytochrome P450.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Materials and Methods specify S. lycopersicum cv. Micro-Tom.

Applied_Species.reason: The knockout and expression analyses were performed in tomato.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is explicitly Solanum lycopersicum.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The applied tomato plants were cv. Micro-Tom.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: SlCYP722C was studied in the context of strigolactone biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: The gene is linked specifically to orobanchol formation, i.e., the canonical SL/orobanchol branch.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The data place SlCYP722C downstream of CLA and at the BC-ring closure/canonical SL-forming step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Recombinant SlCYP722C catalyzed the same reaction as VuCYP722C, which used CLA as substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper supports formation of orobanchol and 2'-epi-orobanchol by SlCYP722C via 'same reaction as VuCYP722C,' and knockout abolishes solanacol accumulation in exudates, but solanacol is not shown as a direct enzymatic product of SlCYP722C.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper states SlCYP722C catalyzed the same reaction as VuCYP722C, i.e., direct conversion of CLA to orobanchol without passing through 4DO.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: SlCYP722C directly forms the target product orobanchol in vitro.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports that CYP722C is a key enzyme/novel orobanchol synthase, but it does not explicitly establish rate limitation or control-step kinetics.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: The terminal product directly linked to SlCYP722C is orobanchol.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Orobanchol is a canonical strigolactone, and SLs are described as carotenoid-derived apocarotenoid signals/hormones.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper links tomato root exudate SLs including orobanchol/solanacol to rhizosphere signaling and parasitic weed seed germination; SLs are also described broadly as AMF signals.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Orobanchol was analyzed in tomato root exudates.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: SlCYP722C expression was up-regulated in tomato roots under Pi-deficient conditions.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The same tomato root Pi-deficiency induction supports this field.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The relevant treatment was phosphate deficiency in hydroponic culture.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: As with VuCYP722C, microsomal fractions were used for recombinant assays, but native localization in tomato was not directly demonstrated.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Arabidopsis NADPH-CYP reductase was explicitly used in the in vitro assay system for CYP enzymes including SlCYP722C.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by heterologous expression/in vitro assay, CRISPR-Cas9 knockout, LC-MS/MS profiling, and qRT-PCR.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Knockout plants lacked orobanchol and solanacol in exudates, accumulated CLA, and showed architecture comparable to WT.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Methods explicitly state gRNA transcription was enhanced from the U6 promoter.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All five summary points match the Results and Methods: Pi-deficiency induction, same enzymatic reaction as VuCYP722C, KO loss of orobanchol/solanacol with CLA accumulation, unchanged upstream gene expression/architecture, and reduced parasitic weed germination stimulation.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The Discussion explicitly proposes CYP722C-KO plants as a strategy to combat root parasitic weeds without major architecture change and notes normal fruit/seed production in a T1 line.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: The paper states no prominent shoot branching or stem-length defect and suggests no serious yield loss based on normal fruit and seed production, while also implying further evaluation is needed.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper supports CLA release from KO roots and near-normal architecture, and interprets canonical SLs like orobanchol/solanacol as important rhizosphere signals in this context.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Results explicitly state two MAX1 homologs are recorded and Vigun09g224400 was selected as VuMAX1.

Enzyme_Name.reason: VuMAX1 is explicitly a MAX1 homolog, i.e., cytochrome P450 MORE AXILLARY GROWTH 1.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: MAX1 homologs are CYP711A family cytochrome P450s; the paper contrasts VuMAX1 with other MAX1 homologs/CYP711As.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: The cowpea material used was cv. B301.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied species is cowpea, Vigna unguiculata.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: The applied cowpea material was cv. B301.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: VuMAX1 was characterized as part of strigolactone biosynthesis.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: VuMAX1 acts before orobanchol formation, in the upstream common pathway leading to CLA.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: The enzyme converts CL to CLA, placing it upstream/midstream relative to the terminal orobanchol-forming step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Recombinant VuMAX1 catalyzed conversion of CL to CLA.

Primary_Product.reason: CLA is explicitly the product formed by recombinant VuMAX1 from CL.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper states VuMAX1 converted CL to CLA but did not convert CLA to 4DO or orobanchol.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: VuMAX1 forms CLA, the immediate precursor for downstream orobanchol formation.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The entry states 'not established,' which is faithful because the paper does not claim VuMAX1 is rate-limiting.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: VuMAX1 is discussed in the pathway leading ultimately to orobanchol.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Orobanchol is a strigolactone and SLs are described as carotenoid-derived signals/hormones.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper supports SL roles in rhizosphere signaling and parasitic weed germination, which applies to downstream canonical SLs such as orobanchol.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Orobanchol was measured in cowpea root exudates.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: VuMAX1 was among genes up-regulated in cowpea roots under Pi-deficient conditions.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: The same cowpea result supports this field.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The relevant environmental factor was phosphate deficiency.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: The enzyme was assayed using microsomal fractions in vitro, but native localization of VuMAX1 in cowpea was not directly shown.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: Arabidopsis NADPH-CYP reductase was explicitly used in the reconstituted CYP assay.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Supported by qRT-PCR, heterologous expression in insect cells, and in vitro LC-MS/MS assays.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: The data show VuMAX1 provides CLA precursor but is insufficient for CLA-to-orobanchol conversion.

Omics_Data.reason: Cowpea RNA-seq dataset DRA008222 exists, but VuMAX1 itself is not explicitly described as identified from that omics dataset; its expression was assayed by qRT-PCR and it was selected from annotated homologs.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four summary points are directly described in Results: selection of Vigun09g224400 as VuMAX1, conversion of CL to CLA, lack of conversion of CLA to 4DO/orobanchol, and implication that another CYP is responsible.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: The paper does not discuss pathway engineering utility of VuMAX1 as an application.

Other_Important_Info.reason: VuMAX1 was directly tested as part of excluding MAX1/CYP711A as the CLA-to-orobanchol catalyst in cowpea.
